title: Abstract
content: The transparent Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin film materials having two different types of hexagonal and cubic pore structures with a thickness of about 50 m and a lateral size of a few centimeters were successfully synthesized by the solvent evaporation method from tetramethoxysilane (TMOS), vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMOS), titanium tetra(iso-propoxide) (TIP), and octadecyltrimethylammonium chloride (C 18 TAC).The films were converted to Ti-containing nanoporous silica thin films by subsequent calcinations in air at 823 K, while the highly ordered mesostructures and macroscopic morphology were retained after the surfactant removal by calcination.The films with hexagonal and cubic symmetry were obtained by changing the composition of the starting mixtures.Various spectroscopic measurements of these films clearly showed that Ti ions are present in the silica networks as a tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species with a high dispersion state.Thus, developed transparent Ti-containing thin films were used as photocatalysts for the reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to evaluate their unique and high photocatalytic activity.UV irradiation of these films in the presence of CO 2 and H 2 O led to the formation of CH 4 and CH 3 OH as well as CO and O 2 as minor products, their yields increasing linearly against irradiation time, indicating that these films operate as efficient photocatalysts to proceed such a difficult reaction catalytically at 323 K. Furthermore, the films having hexagonal pore structure exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than the Ti-MCM-41 powdered catalyst even with the same pore structure.

title: Experimental
content: Tetramethoxysilane (TMOS), vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMOS), octadecyltrimethylammonium chloride (C 18 TAC) and methanol were purchased from Tokyo Kasei Industries Co., and used without further purification.Ti-tetra(iso-propoxide) (TIP) was purchased from Kanto Chemical Industries Co. and used as received. Ti-containing nanoporous silica films were synthesized in a method similar to that described previously for the synthesis of self-standing nanoporous silica film 50.A typical synthetic procedure for the self-standing films was as follows: 0.98 g of TMOS, 0.32 g of VTMOS, 0.05 g of TIP, 0.38 g of C 18 TAC, and 0.1 g of methanol, ((TMOS VTMOS):TIP:C 18 TAC:methanol 1:150:18:125, and TMOS:VTMOS 7:1) were mixed by magnetic stirring and the mixture was allowed to react under acidic conditions by the addition of 0.1 ml of 1 M HCl for 10 min at room temperature.In order to hydrolyze the alkoxides, deionized water was added to the mixture.When the waterSi ratio was 13 and 43, hereafter, they are referred to as Ti-PS-h-x (x is the SiTi ratio, i.e. 25 or 50) and Ti-PS-c-x, respectively, in which "h" and "c" refer to hexagonal and cubic, respectively.The amount of water was varied to control the mesostructures of the products.Then, the resulting solution was deposited on a poly(ethylene) film and dried in air at 333 K for 1 day to remove the solvent and to complete condensation of the alkoxides.The dried films could then be peeled off from the substrate, yielding flexible, self-standing, and transparent films.The rapid evaporation of the solvents before gelation is important to obtain highly ordered mesostructured materials 678.The trifunctional organoalkoxysilane, VTMOS, suppressed the gelation to give self-standing films with a thickness of several tens of millimeters 19.The as-synthesized films were calcined in air at 823 K for 5 h with a heating rate of 5 K min -1 in order to remove surfactants and obtain porous films. XRD patterns were obtained on a RAD IB diffractometer (Rigaku) using monochromatic Cu K radiation operated at 30 kV and 30 mA.The self-standing films were placed on a glass substrate for the XRD measurements.SEM of the films were obtained on a HITACHI S-2840N scanning electron microscope.TEM of the films were obtained on a HITACHI H8100 TEM with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.IR spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu FT-8200PC or a JASCO FTIR-7300 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer.TG-DTA curves were obtained on a Rigaku TAS-2000 instrument at a heating rate of 10 K min -1 and a-alumina as the standard.The N 2 adsorptiondesorption isotherms for the calcined films were measured at 77 K on a BELSORP 28 instrument (Bell Japan Inc.).Prior to the measurements, the samples were dried at 473 K under vacuum for 3 h.The XAFS spectra (XANES and FT-EXAFS) were measured at the BL-9A facility of the Photon Factory at the National Laboratory for High-Energy Physics, Tsukuba.Si(1 1 1) double crystals were used to monochromatize the X-rays from the 2.5 GeV electron storage ring.The Ti K-edge absorption spectra were recorded in the transmission or fluorescence mode at 295 K.The photoluminescence spectra of the thin films were measured at 77 K using a SPEX FLUOLOG-3 spectrophotofluorometer 38. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O was carried out with the catalyst (50 mg) in a quartz cell with a flat bottom (88 cm 3 ) connected to a con-ventional vacuum system (10 -4 Pa range).Prior to photoreactions and spectroscopic measurements, the catalysts were heated in O 2 at 723 K for 8 h, and evacuated at 473 K for 2 h.UV irradiation of the catalyst in the presence of CO 2 (36 mol) and gaseous H 2 O (180 mol) was carried out using a 100 W high-pressure Hg lamp at 323 K.The reaction products collected in the gas phase were analyzed by gas chromatography.The number of incident photons to the cell was determined by means of a potassium ferrioxalate actinometer.The difference between the number of photons passing through the Ti-containing porous silica thin films and the corresponding number with the pure silica thin films can provide the number of the photons absorbed by the Ti-oxide moiety.Thus, the real quantum yield for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to produce CH 4 and CH 3 OH could be determined 44,45,49.

title: Results and discussion
content: Ti-containing silica-surfactant mesostructured materials were successfully obtained as transparent self-standing films with a thickness of about 50 mm and a lateral size of a few centimeters.It was found that depending on the synthetic conditions such as the amount of water added, films with different mesostructures were obtained.When the waterSi ratio in the precursor solution was below 23, the XRD patterns of the products showed a single diffraction peak (d value of 3.5 nm) with a second order reflection 17,20,23.Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized films prepared at a waterSi ratio of (a) 13 and (c) 43, and their corresponding films (b) and (d), respectively, after calcination at 823 K.As can be seen in Fig. 2(a), the TEM image of the product clearly shows the hexagonal ordered pore arrangements.Since the films were thought to be composed of cylindrical surfactant aggregates oriented parallel to the film plane, (1 1 0) and (2 1 0) reflections of the hexagonal phase are absent in the XRD pattern shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b) 17,20,23.Also, the TEM observations of these Ti-containing materials showed that these materials possess highly ordered hexagonal mesostructures.The controlled amounts of water in the starting mixture were found to contribute to the formation of highly ordered mesostructures, being in good agreement with the fact that the rapid solvent evaporation is known to be a dominant factor in the present synthesis 16,17. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d), the XRD patterns of the films prepared at a waterSi ratio of 43 exhibit diffraction peaks attributable to a cubic symmetry.The formation of the cubic mesostructure can be confirmed by the TEM observa- tions shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c).Thus, self-standing films with two different mesostructures were successfully synthesized and the mesostructures were successfully controlled by the experimental conditions.It is known that different phases could be obtained depending on the synthesis conditions, principally, the silicasurfactant ratios in the starting solution 14,22.In the present system, the added amount of water controlled the mesostructures.When the waterSi ratios were 43 and 2, mesostructured materials with a cubic symmetry were obtained.Hexagonal mesostructured materials were formed when the waterSi ratios were 23 and 12.It is expected that the added water may affect the condensation of the siloxanes to vary the effective volume ratios of the silicasurfactant.Further increase and decrease in the ratios of waterSi may cause the precipitation of Ti-oxides and insufficient polymerization of alkoxysilanes, respectively. The TG curves of the products showed their weight losses at around 572-672 K which accompanied exothermic peaks in the corresponding DTA curves.Since the reaction at high temperatures was the oxidative decomposition of surfactant as well as that of the vinyl group of VTMOS, it seemed possible that the films were transformed into porous silica.Accordingly, the as-synthesized films were calcined in air at 823 K for 3 h in order to remove the surfactant from the materials.These processes could be observed by the IR spectra of the calcined products, where the absorption bands attributed to surfactant gradually disappeared during the calcination.The IR spectra of the calcined products showed that vinyl groups of VTMOS also completely disappeared during the calcination.The SEM observations of the as-obtained films clearly showed that these films were smoothly continuous and crack free.In order to remove vinyl groups in the resulting nanoporous silica films, the surfactant extraction from the as-synthesized products was attempted by using ethanol and water as solvents.However, the removal of surfactant resulted in a complete loss of the mesostructures.Thus, at the present stage, it seems that the thermal decomposition of the surfactant is the best way for surfactant removal. As mentioned above, the XRD patterns of the films calcined at 823 K are shown in Fig. 1(b) and(d).Sharp diffraction peaks can be seen in the XRD patterns of the these films, showing that the ordered hexagonal and cubic mesostructures were retained even after the removal of surfactant.The repeat distance due to the wall decreases with the calcination at high temperature due to the removal of the surfactant and the condensation of the SiOH groups.For the hexagonal films, the d(1 0 0) value decreases to 2.8 nm (Fig. 1(b)).The d values of the cubic silica-surfactant film calcined at 823 K were 3.36, 3.10, and 2.81 nm, respectively (Fig. 1(d)). N 2 adsorptiondesorption isotherms of the films calcined at 823 K were also measured.The isotherms showed that the BET surface area of the hexagonal and cubic films was about 900 m 2 g -1 .The average pore sizes were estimated from the Horvath-Kawazoe plot 51 derived from the isotherms to be 1.9 and 1.8 nm for the products with the hexagonal and cubic mesostructures, respectively.Thus, the successful formation of self-standing Ti-containing nanoporous silica films with large BET surface area and highly ordered pore arrangements were confirmed.Although the synthesis and applications of Ti-containing mesoporous silica powders have already been documented 5253545556575859, this is the first successful synthesis of Ti-containing nanoporous silica thin films. In the syntheses of zeolites and mesoporous molecular sieves, the morphology of these materials was mainly in powdered form.However, as mentioned above, powdered materials are difficult to handle for practical use as photocatalysts.Therefore, the synthesis of transparent porous silica thin films is a subject of current interest 464748.The synthesized transpar- ent Ti-containing porous silica thin films have a larger surface area and can realize the efficient absorption of UV light, showing that they have potential for use as effective photocatalysts.Fig. 3 shows the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the Ti-containing porous silica thin films.Ti-PS-h-50 and Ti-PS-c-50 exhibit an absorption band in the wavelength region of 200-260 nm.This band can be attributed to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) process involving an electron transfer from O 2-to Ti 4 of the highly dispersed tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species 5,383940414243444560.On the other hand, the Ti-PS-h-25 shows an absorption band in the wavelength region of 250-340 nm and the weak LMCT band due to the presence of tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species.The absorption band at around 250-300 nm suggests the presence of an aggregated octahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species as the major species. Fig. 4 shows the XAFS (XANES (left) and FT-EXAFS (right)) spectra at the Ti K-edge of the Ti-containing porous silica thin films.As can be seen in Fig. 2, both Ti-PS-h-50 and Ti-PS-c-50 materials exhibit an intense single pre-edge peak.Since tetrahedrally coordinated Ti ion such as Ti(OPr i ) 4 is well known to exhibit an intense single pre-edge peak due to the lack of an inversion center in the regular tetrahedron structure 61,62, the observation of this intense single pre-edge peak clearly indicates that the Ti-oxide species in the Ti-containing porous silica thin films has a tetrahedral coordination.Ti-PS-h-50 and Ti-PS-c-50 exhibit the same intensities and positions in their pre-edge peaks, indicating that both films have the same titanium coordination.On the other hand, Ti-PS-h-25 shows lower pre-edge intensity as compared with the other two films and a shift to higher energy level of the edge position, indicating the presence of both a tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species and an octahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species 61. From the curve fitting analysis of the EXAFS spectra, it was determined that the Ti-oxide species of Ti-PS-h-50 and Ti-PS-c-50 exists in tetrahedral coordination with a Ti-O bond distance of 1.82 and 1.83 A, respectively.The coordination number and Ti-O bond distance of Ti-PS-h-25 were determined to be 5.0 and 1.86 A, respectively, indicating the presence of an aggregated octahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species and a tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species.These results are in good agreement with the results obtained from UV-Vis absorption investigations shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 5 shows that Ti-containing porous silica thin film (Ti-PS-h-50) exhibits a photoluminescence spec- quenching Photoluminescnce yields a.u.trum in the wavelength region of 400-600 nm having a peak at around 485 nm by the excitation of the LMCT band of the tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species at around 200-260 nm at 77 K.The observed photoluminescence can be attributed to the radiative decay process from the charge transfer excited state to the ground state of the highly dispersed tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxides located in silica matrices of the thin films 38394041424344454647484950515253545563.As shown in Fig. 5, the addition of H 2 O or CO 2 molecules on to the Ti-containing porous silica thin film leads to an efficient quenching of the photoluminescence.The lifetime of the charge transfer excited state was found to be shortened by the addition of CO 2 or H 2 O, its extent depending on the amount of added molecules.Such an efficient dynamic quenching of the photoluminescence with CO 2 or H 2 O clearly suggests not only that the tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species locates at the positions accessible to the added CO 2 or H 2 O molecules, but also that the added CO 2 or H 2 O interacts andor reacts with the Ti-oxide species in both its ground and excited states. In fact, UV irradiation of the Ti-containing porous silica thin films in the presence of CO 2 and H 2 O led to the formation of CH 4 and CH 3 OH as the main products as well as O 2 , CO, C 2 H 4 as the minor products 474849.As can be seen in Fig. 6, the yields of photoformed CH 4 and CH 3 OH increase linearly against the irradiation time, indicating that the reaction proceeds photocatalytically.These products were neither detected under dark conditions nor with UV irradiation of the mesoporous silica thin films without Ti-oxides.These results clearly indicate that the presence of both Ti-oxides included within the silica framework as well as UV irradiation are indispensable for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to take place on the films.Fig. 7 shows the rates of the formation of CH 4 and CH 3 OH in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O on various types of Ti-containing porous silica thin films.It can be seen that Ti-PS-h-50 film exhibits higher photocatalytic activity than the powdered Ti-MCM-41 even with the same pore structure.Moreover, the powered Ti-PS-h-5, which were prepared by grounding the film to powder forms, still maintains higher photocatalytic activity as compared with the powdered Ti-MCM-41.These results indicate that the high photocatalytic activity of Ti-PS-h-50 is attributed to an efficient absorption of UV light due to its high transparency 47.Ti-PS-h-25 film involving an aggregated octahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species exhibits a high selectivity for the formation of CH 4 , as with the case of the bulk powdered TiO 2 photocatalysts.Ti-PS-c-50 film shows higher selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH, though its photocatalytic activity is lower than that of Ti-PS-h-50 film.In powdered form, the effect of the scattering of light on the particle surfaces would be large so that effective light absorption and measurement may not be realized.It is, therefore, a great advantage to use transparent Ti-containing porous silica thin films as photocatalysts to measure the exact quantum yield of the photocatalytic reaction using chemical actinometers 47.Fig. 8 shows the quantum yield of the various Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films as well as the Ti-oxides anchored on to the porous silica glass (PVG), i.e.TiPVG.In contrast to the quantum yield observed with the transparent TiPVG, the quantum yields observed with transparent Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films exhibit much higher quantum yields.Such improvement in the photocatalytic activity of Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films can be attributed to their larger surface area and higher transparency as compared with TiPVG photocatalyst.These results clearly suggest that the photocatalytic activity of the transparent Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films is quite high and sufficiently effective for utilization in environmentally harmonious functional photocatalytic materials. The relationship between the selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH and the amount of the surface OH groups of the photocatalysts was investigated.It was found that Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films having small amounts of surface OH groups exhibit the highest selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH.In general, large amounts of H 2 O molecules can adsorb on the surface of the catalysts having a high concentration of surface OH groups.It can, therefore, be assumed that the relative concentration of the H 2 O molecules surrounding the tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species influences the selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH.In a previous study, in the case of high values for the H 2 OCO 2 ratio, the selectivity for the formation of CH 4 became higher 49,64.These results indicate that in the case of high concentrations of surface OH groups, the formation of CH 4 occurred mainly due to the high concentration of the H 2 O molecules surrounding the tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species and the easy interaction of the H 2 O molecules with the excited state of the tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species.In the case of low concentration of surface OH groups, the selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH may be higher since the reaction for the formation of CH 4 is inhibited.Moreover, with the Ti-oxide-based photocatalysts having both aggregated octahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species and a high concentration of surface OH groups, these two main factors being the characteristic feature of bulk powdered TiO 2 photocatalysts and the easy interaction of the H 2 O molecules with the active sites, affect the selectivity in the reaction, resulting in the high selectivity for the formation of CH 4 .

title: Conclusions
content: Transparent self-standing thin films of Ti-containing mesoporous silica materials having a thickness of about 50 m and a lateral size of a few centimeters were successfully synthesized from TMOS, VTMOS, TIP, and C 18 TAC by the solvent evaporation method and subsequent calcinations in air at 823 K.These Ti-containing mesoporous materials possess periodical pore arrangements with hexagonal and cubic symmetries, these mesostructures being controlled by the composition of the starting mixtures.These Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films exhibited quite higher photocatalytic activity than the powdered Ti-MCM-41 catalyst for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O at 323 K. High quantum yields observed with these films could be attributed to their high transparency.Moreover, Ti-oxide containing mesoporous photocatalysts having small amounts of surface OH groups led to a high selectivity for the formation of CH 3 OH.Thus, these high transparent Ti-containing thin films having a highly dispersed tetrahedrally coordinated Ti-oxide species in their silica networks were found to be good as efficient photocatalysts for various significant reactions that address the environmental concerns. Fig. 1.XRD patterns of the as-synthesized self-standing Ticontaining mesoporous films prepared at the waterSi ratios of (a) 13 and (c) 43 and their corresponding films (b) and (d), respectively, calcined at 823 K. Fig. 2. Transmission electron micrographs of the Ti-containing mesoporous films prepared at a waterSi ratio of (a) 13; (b) and (c) 43. Fig. 3. UV-Vis absorption spectra of the Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films. Fig. 4. Ti K-edge XANES (left) and FT-EXAFS (right) spectra of the Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films.CN: coordination number; R: Ti-O bond distance (A). Fig. 5. Photoluminescence spectrum of (a) the Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin film (Ti-PS-h-50); (b) the effect of the addition of CO 2 : 0.15 mmol g -1 catalyst; (c) H 2 O: 0.15 mmol g -1 catalyst and (d) H 2 O: 1.5 mmol g -1 catalyst molecules on the photoluminescence spectrum at 77 K. Excitation at 260 nm. Fig. 6.Reaction time profiles of the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 (36 mol) with H 2 O (180 mol) to produce CH 4 and CH 3 OH on the Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin film at 323 K. Fig. 7. Rates of CH 4 and CH 3 OH formation in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O on (a) Ti-PS-h-25; (b) Ti-PS-c-50; (c) Ti-MCM-41; (d) the powdered form of Ti-PS-h-50 and (e) Ti-PS-h-50.Reaction time: 6 h; intensity of light: 265 W cm -2 . Fig. 8.Quantum yields of the product formation (CH 4 CH 3 OH) in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O on Ti-containing mesoporous silica thin films and also on the Ti-oxides anchored on to the transparent porous silica glass, TiPVG.
